title: Terra sigillata burnishing on air-dry clay
subtitle: Polishing a thin terra sigillata layer to a soft satin sheen on bone-dry air-dry work
excerpt: Terra sigillata is an ultra-fine clay slip that, when brushed onto a near-bone-dry surface and polished with a soft material while still damp, fires or sets to a soft satin sheen with no glaze. The same technique adapts to air-dry clay: brush three thin coats, burnish between each, and the finished surface holds a quiet polished glow.
sourceNotes: Terra sigillata is the classical Roman and Greek decorative slip, recovered as a technique in twentieth-century studio pottery. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry, for the historical background. Mortimer Wheeler's archaeological reports for the Roman-era surface reference. This tutorial adapts the burnishing principle to air-dry clay.

glossaryTerms.definition: An ultra-fine refined clay slip made by settling out the largest particles from a clay-water mixture. When polished on a near-dry surface, it sets to a soft satin sheen.
Compressing and polishing a clay surface with a smooth material (a soft cloth, a plastic bag, a polished stone) to align the fine particles and produce a low sheen.
Air-dried but unfired. For terra sigillata burnishing, the surface should be just before bone-dry: still cool to the touch, but holding shape firmly.

content.text: Terra sigillata

is the Latin name for sealed earth, the classical name for the polished red slipware of Roman and Greek tableware. The slip itself is the finest fraction of a clay-water mix, settled out over a day or two so only the smallest particles remain. Brushed onto a near-dry clay surface and polished with a soft material, those particles lie flat and reflect light evenly. The finished surface has a soft, satin sheen, deeper than matte but not glossy.

The technique works on air-dry clay using ready-mixed terra sigillata or one made at home from a small batch of air-dry clay sieved through a fine cloth. No firing is needed. The finished surface is decorative only and stays porous, like the air-dry clay underneath.
When to use it

Burnished terra sigillata suits flat or gently curved surfaces where the polished sheen reads clearly: tiles, small slab dishes, pinch pots, coil-built vases. Highly textured surfaces lose the polish in the recesses and look streaky. The technique is intermediate because timing matters: the surface must be near
bone-dry
but not fully dry, and the slip must be polished while it is still slightly damp.
What you need
An air-dry clay piece, dried to nearly bone-dry: still cool to the touch but holding shape firmly under pressure
Terra sigillata slip, red or white (about 50 ml for a small piece)
A soft full-bellied mop brush
A smooth, soft burnishing material: a strip cut from a thin plastic bag, the back of a spoon, or a piece of clean soft chamois leather
Step-by-step
1. Check the surface dryness

The piece should feel cool against the cheek and look uniform in colour, with no darker damp patches. If the surface is still very damp, the slip will not stick. If the surface is fully bone-dry and dusty, sponge it lightly and let the moisture even out for ten minutes before brushing.
2. Brush the first thin coat

Load the mop brush with terra sigillata and lay down one thin even coat across the area to be polished. Work in one direction without backbrushing. The coat should be just thick enough to wet the surface; thicker coats crack as they dry.
3. Wait until the surface dulls

Watch the surface as it dries. When the wet shine disappears and the slip turns from glossy to matte, the moment to burnish has arrived. This is usually three to five minutes after brushing, depending on room conditions. If a fingertip pressed gently leaves a mark but no wet smear, the timing is right.
4. Polish with the burnishing material
Lay a strip of thin plastic flat against the slipped surface and rub in small, firm, overlapping circles.
Burnishing

presses the fine particles together and brings a soft sheen out of the slip. Work each section until the sheen is even, then move on. Pressure should be steady, not hard; pressing too hard scrapes the slip off entirely.
5. Repeat the coat-and-polish for two more layers

Three coats is the standard. The first builds the slip layer, the second deepens the colour, the third refines the sheen. Brush, wait for the dulling moment, polish. Each layer should be thinner than feels intuitive; thick layers craze and flake.
What it looks like when it is right

The finished surface has a soft satin glow, somewhere between matte and gloss. Light catches the surface without reflecting sharply. The colour reads richer than the bare clay underneath: red terra sigillata gives a warm terracotta tone, white sigillata gives a creamy ivory. The polish is most visible when the piece is tilted in raking light.
Common mistakes
Polishing too early. If the slip still looks wet, the burnisher drags it across the surface and smears the colour. Wait for the matte dull moment before working.
Polishing too late. Slip that has dried fully resists the burnisher and the sheen never develops. The window between wet and dry is about two minutes.
Coats too thick. Crazing (a network of fine cracks) appears as the slip dries. Thinner coats avoid this. Build the sheen by layering, not by piling on slip.
Burnishing too hard. Pressing aggressively scrapes the slip off rather than polishing it. Steady, light, repeated strokes work better than a single hard pass.

items.symptom: Sheen is patchy across the surface
items.cause: Some areas polished at the right moment, others too early or too late
items.fix: Work in small sections, brushing and polishing one before moving to the next; the timing window is short

items.symptom: Slip flakes off in dry patches
items.cause: Coats too thick, or surface too dry before the slip was brushed
items.fix: Sponge the bare clay lightly before re-brushing; lay thinner coats and let each dull only briefly before polishing

items.symptom: Final surface stays matte, no sheen
items.cause: Polish was applied with the wrong material, or pressure was too light
items.fix: Use thin plastic (a freezer-bag strip is ideal) or chamois leather; press firmly enough to feel the surface compressing
